sentences.text: Yes.
--coming back to the ghetto.
Yeah, from the-- he was-- his fingernails and toenails were burned with candles.
A week after that, we were suddenly ordered to leave everything behind, that they were now resettling us in Hungary in a labor camp.
And the reason they said that we should leave everything behind, because the place we were going to will have everything we need.
We were crowded into cattle cars, I think up to 90, if I am correct.
It was very crowded.
The cattle car doors were sealed.
They had little bar wire windows.
And by each cattle car, there was a Hungarian gendarme on a platform.
God forbid we could get out from those little windows, he would shot us.
The train started moving extremely fast-- click, clack, click, clack.
We just could hear the sound of the wheels moving.
It would sometimes stop.
And they would fill in the steam engine with water.
And my father was the commandant of our cattle car.
I remember him asking the gendarme, the Hungarian gendarme, for water, because we were very thirsty.
And he would say, 10 gold watches.
So they would pass 10 gold watches through the barbed wire.
And he-- he must've stood outside throwing in some buckets of water through the barbed wires.
And we tried to catch some.
I never got even a drop.
And then the train would move on.
And that scene was repeated for about three and  a half days.
The last station the train stopped, it was nighttime.
And, again, my father asked for water.
And the answer came in German.
And there was a dead silence in the car.
Suddenly, everybody realized that we have crossed into Germany, and the end was near.
People started praying, crying.
And then the train moved again in the next stop.
It was still dark.
My father asked for water.
Nobody even answered.
But you could hear a lot of footsteps outside running back and forth, Germans yelling orders.
That went on probably for about an hour.
We couldn't see anything.
There was dark.
And-- and my father gathered us in the corner of the cattle car and said, children, I don't know what's happening, where we are.
But if any of you survive this terrible war, please make sure that you're going to go to Palestine, because I believe that you have an uncle there by the name of Aaron.
And I believe the Jews can live there in peace and freedom.
And we promised.
With tears in our eyes, we promised.
I realized my father never even spoke to us as grown ups before.

I-- he opened his prayer book like nothing happened-- tried to figure out the direction-- which direction was east, and began his morning prayers with his [NON-ENGLISH] and his tallit, and everything, in the midst of crying and screaming children.
And many other Jews joining in were praying to God probably for the last time to help them.
I was angry.
It looked so hopeless.
It-- you could-- we knew we were in Germany.
We didn't know where.
Finally, the cattle car door slid open and this mass of people poured out.
We stepped down.
My mother grabbed my sister and me by the  hand.
And I was trying to look around to see where on earth-- where we-- what is that place?
What does it look like?
Everything looked gray, lifeless, huge barbed wires, the smell.
And suddenly, I realized that my father and my two older sisters disappeared in the crowd.
I never, ever saw them again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] My mother was holding on to us.
And  I think that she felt that as long as she could hold on to us, that she could protect us.
We were-- we didn't move anywhere.
We were practically frozen in place.
SS were running up and down the platform yelling [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
We didn't budge.
We didn't move.
We didn't follow the-- the-- the crowd.
I don't know how she kept standing there.
An SS came and demanded to know if we were twins.
And my poor mother didn't know if that was good or bad.
What should she say?
What-- what-- what was this place?
What was going on here?
And she hesitantly asked, is that good?
And the SS nodded yes.
And she said, yes, they are twins.
At that moment, an SS came, grabbed her to the right.
We were pulled to the left.
We were crying and screaming.
And I looked back, and i saw my mother hands stretched out toward us.
I never said goodbye to her.
Of course, I didn't know that this was going to be the last time I would see her.
We were taken to the side of the railroad track where they were gathering, I guess, all the twins, because there about five or six sets of twins.
And we waited around for quite some time, maybe half an hour, another hour.
I don't know.
I looked around.
I could see the platform.
There probably was a selection platform.
A lot of commotion there.
A guy that looks tall and very elegantly dressed and was kind of doing something like that, pointing, [POINTING] dividing people.
I had no idea what all that meant.
Finally, we were marched.
In our group, there were about seven or eight sets of twins.
We were under SS supervision.
An SS was-- one or two SS were accompanying us.
And there was one grown up, one mother.
And I could never really figure out how she got to even stay with her children.
Her name was Mrs.
Changeri.
She lives in Israel.
She's still alive.
And we were going through this very tall barbed wire, and finally, from one camp into another, and were taken into a building that-- was a brick building.
Was huge.
We were ordered to undress.
Our clothes were taken away.
And we were sitting on bleachers.
Quite frankly, I don't think that any human being can ever be prepared for a place like Auschwitz, no matter how much persecution before then.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought for a while  it seemed like I am watching something that-- it's not really relating to me.
I was numb.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I closed my eyes.
And I said, maybe this is a nightmare.
And if I close them and open them up again, maybe the whole thing will disappear.
It didn't.
We sat there naked in the company of complete strangers for the better part of the day.
Later on in the afternoon, we were lined up for registration and tattooing.
They cut our braids down.
And Mrs.
Changeri's head was shaved.
And the so-called barber explained that we are privileged because we are twins.
And we are permitted to have our own hair.
Then we were lined up for registration and tattooing.
And when my turn came, I decided that I was going to fight back, that I was not going to let them do to me whatever they wanted without fighting back.
And so they started the number.
And I began screaming and kicking.
My number never came out clear.
Four SS-- four-- two SS and two prisoners, women prisoners, had to restrain me.
And I said to myself, well, I need to find some kind of an excuse, because nice girls does-- do not do that.
And I said, I will cooperate with you if you bring my mother here.
And I remember, they said, well, you could see her tomorrow.
And I could think, really, how on earth will they reunite us tomorrow when they just stripped me apart from her earlier this morning?
That-- it seemed to be so-- such a bold lie that  I couldn't really accept it.
My-- unfortunately, my poor sister believed it.
She kept waiting for us to see my mother.
She never told me about it until 1985.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They had a very tough time  with me.
I decided that I was not going to be a very cooperating victim.
And so I kicked, screamed, and it's very difficult, because the way they do that number, they heated a pen like-- it looked like a writing   pen-- old-fashioned writing pen.
They heated it over open flame, then they dipped it into ink.
And then dot by dot, they had to make out the letter A-7063.
Now, if you keep wiggling your arm, then is very difficult to make good number.
Merriam tells me that I bit the SS who was holding my arm.
I still don't remember.
But I was a nice little girl, right?
I wouldn't do something like that.

After registration and tattooing, our clothes were returned to us with a huge oil paint, red cross, painted on the back from shoulder to shoulder from the top of the neck all the way to the bottom of the dress.
I said, why on earth would they ruin our nice dresses with the red paint?
When I took a Holocaust 101 class in 1979, I asked my professor maybe he could help me.
He said, well, you were part of medical experiments.
What did you expect to have on your back?
After that, we were marched between the barbed wire out from that camp.
And it was evening already.
Women prisoners were returning from work.
These were not women.
These were walking human skeletons.
One poor woman saw us and started yelling, children, children, where did you come from?
Hoping, of course, to find something about her own child.
She never found out, because the SS ordered the two huge German shepherds, and they tore her to pieces in front of us.
That picture, unfortunately, was repeated many times during our stay in Auschwitz, because anytime we walked, we always walked under SS supervision.
And people would see us.
They always wanted to find out who we were.
We entered the barrack in Camp A in Birkenau.
And two twins-- I think that they were probably 11 or 12, Hungarians, called Judith and Lea.
They showed us to our bunk beds, which were a skinny, little bunk bed.
We slept in it about four children on occasion when it was very full, at best, two.
But we hardly came in.
Everybody was rushing to the front where the evening meal was being distributed, which was about a two and a half inch slice of very black bread and the liquid everybody called coffee.
Judith and Lea kind of showed us around and said,  well, come and eat.
Merriam and I looked at the bread and we never even discussed it.
And I said, no, we cannot eat this bread.
It's not kosher.
We have not eaten in four days, but we weren't going to eat it.
We were not going to break God's laws.
So we said, well, do you want it?
And they said, sure, and grabbed it, and started laughing.
You're stupid.
They said, you cannot be spoiled here.
If you want to survive, you better learn to eat everything.
It didn't really make to me any sense yet.
After the meal, we all-- they wanted to tell us about the camp.
And they wanted to tell us about did you-- did you-- did you smell the smell, smell that awful smell?
Did you see the huge chimneys?
I didn't really pay attention to them.
So we went to the back of the barrack.
It was already dark outside.
We looked over at the sky, and there were these huge chimneys, and flames were leaping out of it in every direction.
I said, what are they burning so late at night?
And Lea said, well, after every transport that comes, the chimneys burn day and night.
Said, they want to kill all the Jews.
Only the children, the old, the sick, those who cannot work, all end up in the flames.
And I said, well, we are alive, and what are you saying?
We are children.
She said, oh, but we are special.
We are twins.
And Mengele is doing experiments and all.
You are going to meet him tomorrow.
He comes in every morning.
We went to bed.
I don't remember sleeping in four days.
I don't remember eating in four days.
I was completely mentally and physically drained.
It was-- everything like it was happening to somebody else.
And I was laying in that hard wooden bed with a little straw mattress.
It was filthy.
And I couldn't sleep.
So I was tossing and turning.
The barrack was divided in the middle with a bench-like structure that was like two rows of brick.
And it looked like the height of a bench.
At the base of that bench, I saw something moving.
And I kept looking-- started counting 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, started screaming.
Mice!
Mice!
Coming from a farm, I saw a lot of mice, and I was always scared of them.
One of the girls from the top bunk had started yelling at me, stupid!
These are not mice.
These are rats.
I never seen anything so big.
They looked almost like cats.
But  I couldn't go back to sleep.
So Merriam and I went to the latrine.
Was at the end of the barrack, small room, some holes in the ground.
I just entered the place.
I couldn't believe my eyes.
There were the corpses, the naked corpses, of three dead children.
Their eyes were wide open looking at me.
And I became very angry.
I said, why did they die?
Why did they permit themselves to die?
And I made the first silent pledge.
I will do everything in my power to make sure that Merriam and I won't end up on that filthy floor.
I didn't even think in the terms of survival.
I just didn't want to end up like these children.
And I realized that if I wasn't going to take any action.
I was going to end up there.
I didn't know what to do, how to survive in Auschwitz.
There were no rules.
There were no briefings.
There were no-- only rumors and experience.
That's all there was.
I started watching the other girls, other children who had been there a little bit longer.
And I found out that the best way to get any food was to become an organizer.
To organize in the Auschwitz language meant stealing from the Germans.
And the best place to organize was to go to the kitchen and steal some potatoes, or organize some potatoes.
Potatoes seemed to be the miracle food in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They would choose every morning.
Our-- our morning would start, first of all, we would get up.
And the following day, we got up about five o'clock.
We straightened out our bed.
And by six o'clock-- and we helped some of the younger children.
Some of them were only one years old.
There were no mothers in that barrack.
In spite of whatever you read in any books, there were no mothers.
The oldest child was 13.
And we would go outside for roll call.
The roll call lasted anywhere from half an hour to six, and at one occasion, eight hours.
Everybody had to be accounted for regardless of their condition, regardless of the fact if they were alive or dead.
So those three dead children that I saw on the bathroom in the latrine that night, they were placed in front of the group.
After the roll call, the children were taken back, the dead children, and placed in their own bunk beds.
And we would stand by our bunk beds with our hands behind our back like little soldiers for Mengele's inspection.
There was-- we had three supervisors in our barrack [NON-ENGLISH].
And one of them would stand at the front and say, Mengele is coming.
And everybody would just-- it's like-- like some kind of a horror was entering.
He would come very elegantly with his hands behind his back.
He had his stick in his hand.
He would hit it sometimes against his riding boots, and start counting.
He always had at least 6 people with him-- many times as many as 8 or 10.
It was a big entourage.
One of the doctors who was always with him was-- nobody ever talks about him-- Hans Konig.
He was-- nobody ever feared him either.
He was tall, skinny, older than Mengele with graying hair, gold-rimmed glasses.
Clearly remember him.
He would count.
And then he stopped by, saw these dead children, and he began screaming.
Why did you let these children die?
And his voice was penetrating everybody's bone and soul.
I didn't really-- as time went on, I didn't understand why it was such a big deal, because by now, we knew-- within a week, I knew what he was doing.
But I understood much later.
Those children that died in the camp, in the barracks, they spoiled the experiment.
The children that were killed by him did not die in the barracks.
They died in the lab.
Before I ever had a chance to organize any food or try out my organizing skills, I was injected with something that made me very ill.
That was sometime in June.
After one of the visits to the lab, I became extremely ill.
There were two kinds of labs.
And that's an other very long story in itself.
One lab I call the blood lab because basically, what they did-- they would tie both hands with a rubber hose.
And then from this arm, they would take a lot of blood, on occasion, five, six vials, to see how much blood a person can lose and still live.
In this arm, they would give shots-- four, five, six.
I-- I-- occasionally-- I usually could count five needles going into my arm.
My way of coping with it was to turn my head away and just wait for it to end.
After one of these visits to the lab, I became very ill.
And the rumor was that anybody who was taken to the hospital never came back.
I desperately tried to hide the fact that I was ill.
We were outside.
We had a fenced in area by the barrack where we could sometimes supposedly play.
Who wanted to play?
Children who face life and death situations are no longer children, and they do not want to play.
But it was an area that we could sit around, watch the airplanes come.
And they would come daily, sometimes two, three times a day.
The American airplanes would come, would make a huge yellow smoke circle in the sky.
Nobody ever told me, but after a few times, I realized that that meant they  are now going to bomb inside that circle.

And I think that this particular situation that the airplanes were coming to bomb was my only understanding that there was an outside war there that gave me hope that someday we might be free, because one of the most desperate things for us in the camp as children-- we lose-- we lost our point of reference very fast.
I felt for most of the time I was in the camp that the whole world was a concentration camp except for those airplanes that were coming to free us.
And I remember looking up.
And I could see on one of them, the American flag.
And here I was trembling.
And I knew that I was desperately ill.
I did not know at that time that I was injected with something.
I just knew I was very ill, because I had been injected now for weeks and months.
Next visit to the lab, I tried very hard to hide the fact that I was ill.
But instead of tying my arms, they took me to the side room  and measured my fever.
And I knew I was in trouble.
I was taken to the hospital, put into a barrack that I have not met many other people who have been in it.
I call it the barrack of the living dead.
I was put in a small room with two other twins who were a little bit older than me.
And they explained to me that we do not get any food here because this barrack is not even allocated the minimum food that the rest of the barracks are.
That evening, Mengele came in with five other doctors, looked at my fever chart, never examined me, never ran any test.
And declared, laughing, too bad.
She's so young.
She has only two weeks to live.
I made a second silent pledge that I will do anything in my power to prove him wrong to survive and be reunited with Merriam.
For the first two weeks, I was between life and death.
I remember most of the time waking up on  the barrack floor as I was crawling, because I couldn't walk.
And even in semi-conscious state of mind, I kept telling myself, I must survive.
I must survive.
I would fade in and out of consciousness.
I-- I looked up in the two sides of the three-story bunk beds filled with this walking dead, or living dead, because they couldn't walk.
All they could do is scream.
Twice a week, the trucks would come.
And they would throw them like sacks of potatoes onto the truck.
And the screams were horrifying.
So this was the place where they collected people.
They had a place in the gas chamber.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After two weeks, my fever broke.
I realized that if I could show them that my fever was going to go down, that somehow they were going to release me.
So I learned to read the thermometer.
The two girls who were with me taught me how.
And I don't what disease they had.
The-- the nurse came, put in the thermometer.
I would pull it out, check it.
If it was too high, I would shake it down, put it back in, and let it stick out in the back.
And this way I was in charge of how much fever I was going to have.
And I was very concerned, because I said, well, I could right now be well and be reunited with Merriam.
But I was worried that they would become suspicious.
So I worked it very carefully.
It took me three weeks for my fever to be normal, even when I was released.
